section_text: This player is punished in many rounds, often despite contributing fully or more than some peers, and appears to interpret such punishment as illegitimate or hostile. They do not visibly “comply” with punishers by increasing contributions afterward; in fact, repeated punishment is followed by phases of free-riding and, at one point, intense counter-punishment. This indicates that being punished does not induce them to align with any perceived norm; instead, it undermines their willingness to cooperate and increases their readiness to retaliate. However, the retaliation is not targeted in a clear, one-to-one way at specific named punishers—at least not in a pattern that can be reliably inferred from the transcript. This makes their response more consistent with diffuse revenge against the group climate rather than precise tit-for-tat. After expressing retaliation through costly punishment, they do not persist in punishing the same environment in subsequent rounds even though they keep receiving sanctions; this suggests that their anger response is acute rather than chronic, that they are capable of “moving on” behaviorally even if they do not return to stable cooperation. They never show a pattern of “forgiving through compliance” (i.e., adapting contributions upward to avoid further punishment), so their type resists coercion and does not reward aggressors with greater cooperation.

They are never punished in the transcript, so their direct response to being targeted is unknown. Consequently, their attitudes toward identifiable punishers, in terms of forgiveness, retaliation, or compliance when personally punished, cannot be inferred. However, from the fact that they themselves engage in norm-enforcing punishment aligned with the group’s stated cooperative standard, it is plausible—but not confirmable—that they would view justified, contribution-based punishment as legitimate. Whether they would retaliate against perceived unfair punishment or reduce contributions in response is unknown, and confidence about their reactions to personal sanctions is low.

They are punished multiple times by identifiable others but show no direct retaliation and no visible behavioral concession. They continue to contribute fully and continue to speak cooperatively after being punished. They do not confront or call out the punishers in chat, nor do they shift to punishing those who targeted them. Their reaction is essentially non‑reactive: no visible anger, no attempt to negotiate, no attempt to curry favor by lowering contributions or changing strategy. This indicates a forgiving or at least non‑vengeful orientation, and a separation between their own cooperative norm and how others treat them. Whether they internally register the punishment as unfair is unknown; what is clear is that it does not alter their cooperative behavior.

When punished, they do not change their contribution behavior toward greater cooperation; they continue to free-ride consistently. They also do not comment on being personally punished, show no overt emotional reaction, and do not attempt to confront or persuade punishers. The only related remark is a general question about why anyone would pay to deduct others, which reads as a detached critique rather than a plea or threat. They never attempt counter-punishment or public shaming of punishers, and they do not show compliance with the cooperative norm emerging in the group. Overall, they appear resistant to behavioral change under punishment and largely non-reactive at the social level.

They are never punished in the observed interaction, so their behavioral response to being sanctioned is unknown. There is no evidence of anticipatory adjustment specifically aimed at avoiding punishment, nor of any retaliatory tendencies. If punished in the future, it is unclear whether they would respond with compliance (increased cooperation), forgiveness (no change), or counter-punishment; confidence on all such inferences is low.

When punished, this player does not reliably respond with either systematic retaliation or clear compliance. In some instances, being punished coincides with them reducing contributions or shifting target choices in punishment, but the direction of causality is ambiguous: they may already be in a conflictual environment with multiple punishers. They do not launch automatic counter-punishments against any specific punisher, nor do they obviously reward punishers who try to discipline others. Their later behavior toward those who punished them appears mixed and context-dependent rather than rule-based. They neither clearly “forgive and move on” nor lock into persistent vendettas; their stance toward punishers is best described as situational and opaque, with low-confidence inference about underlying rules (unknown whether they mentally track “who started it,” or whether punishment against them shifts their beliefs about group norms).

When another player imposes a deduction on them, they do not alter any aspect of their observable behavior: they continue contributing fully and continue to abstain from both punishment and reward. They do not retaliate against the punisher, nor do they shift toward self-protection by reducing cooperation. This indicates a forgiving or at least non-retaliatory stance. They seem unwilling to use the available tools to discipline or get even, even when personally targeted. There is no sign of compliance to punishment either, since their behavior remains unchanged; instead, they behave as if the punishment is either uninformative, unimportant, or not worth responding to within the mechanism. Their reaction profile fits a “stoic cooperator” who absorbs negative treatment without strategic counter-moves.

The player is never punished in this game, so their response to punishment is unknown. There is no behavioral evidence about whether they would reduce, maintain, or increase their contribution after being punished, nor whether they would retaliate through counter-punishment. Their propensity to forgive, comply with implied norms, or engage in vendettas when personally targeted cannot be inferred from the transcript. Any expectations about their reaction to punishment in future games should be treated as low confidence and unspecified.

This player is resilient and non-retaliatory when punished. After being sanctioned early while personally free-riding, they increase their cooperation and do not attempt to punish back or engage in counter-sanctions, even though punishers are identifiable. This indicates that they accept social feedback and adapt behavior toward the group norm rather than treating punishment as a provocation. They do not use later punishment opportunities to get even with earlier punishers, so they do not appear to follow a vendetta strategy. Their subsequent contributions move toward full cooperation, suggesting that punishment shifts them toward compliance with perceived norms rather than inducing withdrawal or anti-social responses. Their attitude toward punishers seems pragmatic and norm-responsive rather than vengeful; they absorb the cost, infer expectations, and adjust.

Unknown; this player is never punished in the observed interaction, so their reactions to identifiable punishers, including potential retaliation, compliance, or forgiveness patterns, cannot be inferred. Confidence in any prediction here would be low.

The player receives punishment multiple times and continues to free-ride in later decisions, indicating limited compliance with the norm signal. They do not obviously shift into sustained cooperation after being punished; instead, any cooperative episodes are brief and followed by renewed defection. This suggests that punishment does not strongly internalize as a lesson but is treated as a cost of doing business or as something to be occasionally counter-punished. Their own later sanctions against others plausibly include retaliation against those who targeted them, although specific motives are unknown. There is no clear evidence of forgiveness norms or adjustment toward the punishers’ preferences; rather, their behavior fits a “grudging or resistant target” profile, where sanctions may provoke some counteraction but not stable norm compliance. Emotional tone is unknown, but the structural pattern reflects low responsiveness to being disciplined.

This player is punished at least once by someone else, but they do not respond with counter‑punishment or a discernible shift toward spite. They maintain their high contribution and continue to spend heavily on rewards in the same cooperative, group‑focused pattern as before. There is no visible attempt to identify and target the punisher, nor to withdraw cooperation from the group. Their behavior is consistent with absorbing punishment without overt retaliation, suggesting a forgiving or at least non‑confrontational stance. Whether they privately update beliefs about the punisher or feel resentment is unknown, but behaviorally they show no escalation or defection in response. Any norm‑compliance response to being punished is also unclear, because they are already maximally cooperative when the punishment occurs; thus we cannot infer whether punishment would make them “behave better.” Overall, they appear tolerant of being sanctioned and unwilling to let it derail their cooperative/rewarding strategy.

The player is punished once despite having contributed relatively generously. They do not respond with counter-punishment, and they do not sharply reduce their own contributions afterward. This pattern suggests they do not adopt a retaliatory stance, even when the sanction could be perceived as unfair or misdirected. Instead, they maintain their cooperative orientation and refrain from escalating conflict. Their behavior implies either a forgiving or pragmatic attitude: they absorb the cost of being punished without trying to “get even” and without using it as justification to abandon their norm of substantial contribution. Their belief about punishers is unknown, but behaviorally they neither reward them with extra cooperation nor target them with sanctions.

When punished, this player does not clearly become more compliant or consistently more cooperative. Instead, they react in a way that mixes partial persistence, some escalation, and only limited accommodation. They continue to use punishment heavily even after receiving sizeable deductions themselves, indicating low deterrence from being sanctioned. They do not “learn a lesson” in the sense of stopping punishment to avoid counter-punishment. Rather, they show willingness to absorb losses and maintain or even increase their own punitive behavior thereafter. That pattern fits a personality that is stubborn about perceived justice norms and relatively insensitive to payoff losses caused by interpersonal conflict. Their contribution levels after being punished do not systematically shift upward in a sustained way. They sometimes increase contributions following punishment but then later lower them again. This suggests that being targeted does not reliably induce long-term compliance with what punishers might want. They appear to treat punishment more as an attack or as part of a hostile environment than as informative feedback about a stable cooperation norm. Because punishers are identifiable, it is possible they engage in targeted retaliation, but the evidence is uncertain: their overall punishment rate remains high regardless, and the transcript does not reveal specific targets. What can be inferred is that they do not respond with forgiveness or de-escalation; they keep playing a hardline strategy, both in contribution opportunism and in punitive actions. In sum, when punished, this player maintains a confrontational, norm-enforcing stance rather than backing down. They are willing to stay in mutual punishment cycles, accepting reduced earnings, which points to a type that prioritizes enforcing their own fairness standards over avoiding costly conflict.

When they are punished, they do not respond with clear, systematic retaliation or with a stable, submissive adjustment; their reactions are mixed. After receiving punishment, they change their contribution level, and later they both increase and decrease contributions across rounds. This suggests that they notice and integrate punishment as information about norms or others’ expectations, but they do not blindly comply with it. There is no evidence of them punishing back immediately or starting a clear vendetta pattern, despite knowing that punishers are identifiable. This indicates a relatively low tendency toward direct revenge against punishers, or at least an unwillingness to spend heavily just to retaliate. Instead, they seem more focused on their broader fairness standard and on punishing clear free-riding, regardless of whether that comes from someone who previously punished them. Their stance toward punishment from others appears to be: “take it as a noisy but relevant signal” rather than “escalate conflict.” They adjust their own behavior somewhat after being punished, but they also eventually revert to a contribution level that fits their own view of fair cooperation. This points to a personality profile that is moderately forgiving, norm-sensitive, and self-directed: they care about group expectations but ultimately preserve their own cooperative standard rather than fully capitulating to external pressure.

When they are punished, they do not react with visible anger, confrontation, or attempts to identify or retaliate against the punisher, even though punishers are identifiable. They also do not verbally protest or appeal to fairness when they are sanctioned after free-riding or after cooperating. Their chat use does not include any direct addressing of a punisher, and they do not change their subsequent behavior in a clearly compliant way in response to being punished; they continue to mix cooperation and free-riding according to their own pattern. This suggests a high degree of tolerance for sanctions and low concern for vindicating themselves. They neither appease punishers by reliably shifting to higher contributions, nor escalate conflict by counter-punishing. Instead, they treat punishment as part of the payoff environment and maintain their own contribution strategy with minimal emotional or strategic adjustment in direct response to being targeted. Their stance toward punishers can be characterized as detached and non-retaliatory, with no strong evidence of either forgiveness narratives or deliberate compliance-seeking.

They are punished multiple times, especially in rounds where they defect while others cooperate. After receiving punishment, they tend to shift back to full contribution rather than doubling down on defection. This suggests some responsiveness or compliance with the cooperative norm signaled by sanctions. There is no clear pattern of them targeting those who punished them; punishment events against them do not reliably trigger counter-punishment, and they continue to interact with the group in a largely cooperative way. Their later behavior shows no persistent grudges toward specific punishers: they neither single out repeat punishers for future sanctions, nor switch into a persistently non-cooperative mode. Instead, they absorb the cost, adjust their behavior toward cooperation, and then stop being punished. This pattern is consistent with a player who interprets punishment as a signal to conform rather than as a provocation to retaliate. Their stance toward punishers can be described as relatively forgiving and pragmatic: they do not invest much in “teaching the punishers a lesson.” Because chat is disabled, we cannot infer their inner attitudes, but behaviorally they appear to accept the punishment system as part of the game’s structure and adapt rather than fight it.

The player does get punished multiple times and reacts in ways that indicate neither straightforward compliance nor pure appeasement. After being punished, they sometimes continue to cooperate and sometimes defect; there is no consistent shift toward safer, more self-protective behavior in direct response to individual punishers. They also at times increase or maintain their own punishment usage after being punished, which is consistent with retaliatory or confrontational responding rather than deference. Because the mapping of individual punishers and targets is only partially clear, the exact direction of retaliation toward specific players is uncertain, but the overall pattern shows that being punished does not reliably make this player more docile or more cooperative toward the group. Instead, sanctions against them appear to contribute to a more conflictual dynamic, where they are willing to invest in punishment themselves even when it hurts their own payoff. Forgiveness is ambiguous: they sometimes coexist without sanctioning in rounds following punishment, but they also later impose heavy sanctions. This suggests that any forgiveness is conditional and unstable; they neither fully accommodate punishers nor maintain a consistent vendetta against specific individuals.

When others punish them, the player does not respond with visible retaliation. They never punish back, even though punishers are identifiable. Their contribution decisions after being punished do not follow a clear compliance pattern (such as “I was punished for low contribution, so I now contribute more” or vice versa); instead they continue to alternate between full contribution and full free-riding in ways that are not tightly aligned with who punished them or why. This suggests that they neither strongly “submit” to punishment as a learning signal nor treat it as an interpersonal provocation to be avenged. Their stance is closer to indifferent or decoupled: punishment events do not clearly reshape their strategy toward the specific punisher.

They are never punished in this game, so their direct response to being punished is unknown. They observe that punishment is possible and is actually used by another player against a free-rider, but they neither copy that behavior nor comment on it. They also never retaliate against any punisher, even though punishers are identifiable and at least one group member incurs punishment costs. This suggests either indifference to others’ punishment strategies or a preference to stay neutral rather than entering sanction dynamics, but this inference is low confidence because they are never direct targets.

They are never punished by anyone, so there is no observable direct reaction to being sanctioned. They also never punish, so there is no back-and-forth punishment dynamic involving them. As a result, their tendencies regarding forgiveness or retaliatory behavior are unknown. Indirectly, they observe others being punished heavily (e.g., chronic non-contributors and some contributors caught in punishment crossfire), but they do not respond to this by joining punishment or by reducing their own contributions; they appear behaviorally unaffected. From this, with low confidence, they seem relatively indifferent to the social drama around punishment, choosing to continue their own strategy regardless of who is punishing whom.

The player is punished at least twice but never comments on it in chat, does not complain, and does not explicitly retaliate in any identifiable way. Their behavioral changes following punishment are ambiguous in motivation: they increase their own contribution after initially free-riding, but this occurs in a context of social pressure and collective claims of high contribution, not solely in response to being sanctioned. Later, after being punished again, they do not continue or escalate punishment, nor do they engage in visible retaliation. Instead, they quietly adjust their own strategy toward end-game free-riding without overt response to the punisher. This indicates a non-confrontational response style: they absorb sanctions without protest, avoid direct conflict, and do not use chat to challenge or question punishers. Whether they interpret punishment as informative feedback leading to compliance, or as background noise that they simply ignore while optimizing personal payoffs, is unknown; the available behavior suggests at most a weak compliance effect and a higher priority on self-interested adaptation than on retribution.

When punished, the player does not display anger, retaliation, or calls for revenge. They do not adjust their contributions downward, do not use the punishment mechanism in response, and do not publicly confront whoever imposed the cost on them. Their behavior suggests high tolerance of being treated unfairly, or at least a strategic decision to ignore sanctions rather than escalate. They remain focused on cooperation and light norm signaling instead of shifting into a punitive or defensive stance. Their willingness to maintain full cooperation despite being punished indicates that sanctions against them do not readily undermine their prosocial behavior. The specific identity of the punisher(s) and any internal attributions or judgments the player makes about them are unknown.

They are repeatedly punished, sometimes very heavily. In response, they do not reliably conform to the preferences of the punishers by aligning their contributions or sanctions with the majority strategy. Instead, they oscillate: at some points they continue moderate or high contributions even under punishment; at others they sharply defect. Their sanction behavior remains active and often confrontational despite ongoing deductions, suggesting they are not easily cowed by punishment. They do not use chat to confront or negotiate, so their response is expressed purely through contribution changes and counter-punishment. There are signs of retaliatory targeting: they punish players associated with strong pro-punishment talk or previously favored others, indicating that when punishers are identifiable, they may selectively strike back rather than forgive or comply. However, since we cannot perfectly map who punished them, the exact degree of direct tit-for-tat is uncertain (low confidence). Overall, they show low compliance and low verbal engagement, with a tendency toward persistence or escalation rather than accommodation.

When punished themselves, they do not escalate or visibly retaliate. After receiving a nontrivial deduction, they continue to contribute at medium to high levels in subsequent play and maintain their overall cooperative stance. They do not seek out obvious revenge against any identifiable individual; there is no shift toward punishing typical high contributors or toward broadly attacking others. Their behavior suggests they either interpret punishment against them as noise, misdirected enforcement, or part of the game environment rather than a personal affront. They appear relatively forgiving and robust: they absorb punishment and keep focusing on norm enforcement toward clear free‑riders instead of turning punitive energy against whoever hurt them. Whether they update their beliefs about specific punishers is unknown, but they do not show a pattern of personal vendetta.

This player is repeatedly and heavily punished in several rounds, often when they under-contribute relative to others. Despite that, they do not exhibit clear compliance with sanctions. After harsh punishment, they sometimes move to higher contributions, but in other instances they revert to very low contributions again even after it is obvious that the group is willing to punish defectors strongly. They never respond by increasing their own punishment of others in a way that would plausibly qualify as retaliation against those likely to have punished them. Their punishment levels remain low and stable, with no visible spike after they themselves are targeted. This makes vengeful counter-punishment unlikely. Forgiveness vs. resentment is largely unknown due to the absence of chat, but their behavioral pattern suggests they tolerate punishment without becoming a heavy enforcer or obvious retaliator. They also do not clearly conform long-term to cooperative norms despite repeated costly sanctions, which implies either that they discount future punishment heavily, value the option to defect opportunistically, or are experimenting without strongly internalizing the deterrent. In summary, they appear relatively non-retaliatory and only weakly responsive to punishment as a compliance signal.

For most of the game, the player is not targeted by punishment, despite their frequent use of sanctions. When they finally receive a small, identifiable punishment, they do not alter their behavior. They continue to contribute fully and do not respond with counter-punishment against the likely punisher(s), nor do they reduce contributions to protect themselves. This indicates a forgiving or at least non-retaliatory stance. Being punished does not lead to compliance in the sense of becoming more lenient or less cooperative; instead, they stay on their established path of full cooperation plus selective norm enforcement. It also suggests that they do not interpret being punished as a reason to change their target selection. There is no sign they use punishment defensively to deter attacks on themselves. Their primary reference point seems to be their own internal norm about contributions, not direct reciprocity toward punishers.

This player exhibits a striking lack of straightforward compliance with punishment and an absence of systematic retaliatory targeting of punishers. After severe sanctioning and very negative outcomes, they sometimes increase their contributions, sometimes keep them low, and sometimes revert to free-riding; there is no stable, monotonic “I was punished so I will now conform” pattern. This indicates low responsiveness to punishment as a learning signal. They also do not clearly engage in direct revenge: despite being heavily punished across multiple rounds, they do not respond with similarly extreme punishment spending, nor do they escalate over time. Their punishment choices remain bounded and intermittent rather than mirroring the intensity of sanctions they receive. This implies that they are relatively forgiving or at least non-retaliatory at the interpersonal level, even while they continue to violate the group’s contribution norm. In summary, when punished, they neither reliably “shape up” nor lash out at identifiable punishers. Instead, they continue experimenting with contributions and occasionally punishing others, treating incoming punishment more as background noise or a cost of experimentation than as a decisive social signal.

They are punished at least once and do not respond with counter‑punishment at any later point. Since their contribution is always maximal, this punishment is almost certainly antisocial from their perspective. Nonetheless, they do not deviate from full contribution afterward and do not retaliate against identified punishers, despite having that option and visibility. This indicates a forgiving or at least non‑retaliatory stance toward being targeted, and it suggests that their cooperative behavior is not contingent on being treated fairly. Whether they internally adjust their beliefs about those punishers (for example, seeing them as irrational or hostile) is unknown, but behaviorally they neither comply (by reducing contributions) nor retaliate; they simply ignore the sanction and continue as before.

Unknown. The player is never punished by others in the observed game, so there is no behavioral evidence about their response to being sanctioned. Their tendencies toward forgiveness, retaliation, or compliance when they are personally targeted are therefore unknown, and any inference here would be speculative.

When punished, this player does not retaliate, does not shift into a punitive stance, and does not reduce cooperation. Their contributions remain high and their use of punishment remains zero. They also continue to reward others even after being punished. This pattern suggests a forgiving, non-vengeful style. They do not appear to infer a need to change their own behavior in a more selfish or more punitive direction in response to punishment; instead, they continue following their own cooperative and rewarding strategy. Whether they update any beliefs about who is “friendly” or “hostile” is unknown from the data, but their observable response is non-retaliatory and stable.

They are never punished by anyone, so direct evidence of their reactions to punishment is unavailable. It is unknown how they would respond emotionally or strategically if targeted for deductions. Given their consistent full cooperation and non-retaliatory profile in other domains, a reasonable but low-confidence inference is that they would be more inclined to maintain cooperation rather than escalate or reciprocate sanctions. However, any specific claim about forgiveness, retaliation, or norm compliance in response to being punished would be speculative; their response-to-punisher type is effectively unknown.
Unknown. They are never punished in the transcript, so their style of responding to identifiable punishers—whether by forgiving, complying, or retaliating—cannot be inferred.

Unknown. They are never punished, and there is no instance of others being clearly punished and them reacting to it, so their emotional or strategic response to being targeted by identifiable punishers cannot be inferred. Their stated opposition to punishment suggests they might disapprove normatively, but this is low confidence since no actual event tests this.

They are never punished in this game, so their behavioral response to being punished is unknown. There is no evidence about whether they would retaliate, comply, or ignore punishers. Any inference about their reaction to identified punishers would be speculative; confidence on this dimension is low.

They are never punished, so their behavioral or emotional response to punishment is unknown. There is no evidence on whether they would forgive, retaliate, adjust contributions, or target identifiable punishers in return. Given their overall non-punitive and silent style, a hypothesis of low retaliation propensity is plausible but remains low confidence and cannot be confirmed from this transcript.

They are never punished, so their behavioral or emotional response to being sanctioned is unknown. There is no basis to infer whether they would retaliate, comply, or forgive in response to identifiable punishers. Any such tendencies must be treated as unknown or low-confidence.

Unknown. There is no evidence that the player is ever punished by others during the game, so their response to being punished, to identifiable punishers, or to perceived unfair punishment cannot be inferred. Their statements do show an understanding that punishment is costly to the punisher and can be used strategically, but how they react emotionally or behaviorally when personally targeted is not observable.

They are never punished in this game, so direct evidence of how they respond to being punished is unknown. There is no indication of them feeling targeted or expressing resentment toward punishers in general. When others discuss deduction strategies, they treat it as a collective coordination problem and a design feature of the study, not as a personal affront. Given their own moderate, principled use of punishment and their generally calm, cooperative communication style, it is plausible—but low confidence—that if they were punished, they would respond by reassessing their behavior and group norms rather than retaliating. However, their actual retaliation or forgiveness tendencies toward identifiable punishers remain unknown.

They are never punished by anyone in this game (as far as observable outcomes show), so their direct behavioral response to being punished is unknown. They also never act as a punisher, so others do not need to respond to them in that role. Given their general demeanor—friendly, non‑confrontational, and tolerant of free‑riding—it is plausible but low confidence that if punished in another setting, they would seek clarification or treat it as part of the experiment rather than retaliate. However, this is speculative; their actual retaliation threshold, forgiveness tendencies, or likelihood of compliance in response to identified punishers remains unknown.

They absorb large and repeated punishments without any observable behavioral concession, communication, or retaliation toward specific punishers. Despite punishers being identifiable, they never target them, never address them in chat, and do not clearly increase their contributions in a systematic way in response. Periodic increases to full contribution occur, but not in a way that clearly tracks the onset, escalation, or cessation of punishment. This suggests high tolerance for being sanctioned and low sensitivity to social pressure: being punished and collectively criticized does not induce apology, explanation, or consistent compliance. They also do not appear to “teach” punishers a lesson by counter‑punishing. Their response style is essentially passive non‑engagement: they let others spend their own resources punishing them while continuing to pursue their own mixed strategy. Whether they internally register the punishment as significant, misunderstand its impact, or simply do not care about the group’s disapproval is unknown.

They are never punished by others, so their reaction to receiving punishment is unknown. Likewise, because they never punish anyone, their direct interpersonal dynamics with identifiable punishers are also unknown. They neither thank nor criticize punishers, express no approval or disapproval of punitive actions, and offer no strategic feedback. There is no evidence of retaliatory tendencies, reconciliation efforts, or behavioral compliance in response to others’ punishment decisions. In a setting where punishers are identifiable, they simply coexist as a high contributor who does not engage with the punishment system at all. Any propensity to forgive, retaliate, or adjust contributions in response to being punished remains unknown and must not be assumed.

Unknown. The player is never directly punished in the observed interaction, so their reactions to identifiable punishers—whether in terms of emotional response, retaliation, forgiveness, or compliance—cannot be inferred. There is also no indirect evidence (such as commentary about others being punished) that reveals their attitudes toward punishers.

They are punished once by another player after sanctioning that player, yet they do not visibly retaliate. After being punished, they do not direct any punishment back at the punisher, nor do they change their own contribution pattern in a clear retaliatory way. They continue largely with full contributions and only sporadic, norm-based sanctions. There is no explicit chat response acknowledging being punished, no attempt to shame or threaten the punisher, and no attempt to build an opposing coalition. This suggests either indifference to receiving punishment, an acceptance that they may be perceived as harsh and must bear that cost, or simply a focus on maintaining the broader cooperative regime rather than engaging in bilateral feuds. Overall, they appear resistant to getting drawn into tit-for-tat cycles when they themselves are the target.

They are punished multiple times, sometimes substantially, by various others. Their behavioral response is not simple tit-for-tat: they do not consistently retaliate against those who punish them, nor do they clearly reduce or increase contributions in direct reaction to being targeted. On average, their contributions remain in a similar range after being punished, suggesting they do not readily “submit” to punitive pressure by sharply changing their behavior in the desired direction, but they also do not escalate into obvious retaliation. They show some resilience: even after suffering sizable deductions, they continue to contribute moderately or even highly later on. Any specific mapping from “who punished them” to “whom they punish later” is unclear; if retaliation occurs, it is not systematic or strongly legible from the record. Their style toward punishers is best described as tolerant or indifferent rather than vengeful or fully compliant.

They are repeatedly punished by others, sometimes quite heavily. In response, they do not reduce their contribution, do not visibly retaliate against specific punishers in a systematic way, and do not escalate their own punishment intensity. Their cooperative behavior is stable before and after being punished. Their punishment choices do not obviously track “who hit me last;” instead they continue to target apparent under‑contributors or refrain entirely. This suggests a forgiving or at least non‑retaliatory stance: they absorb antisocial or misguided punishment without letting it derail their cooperative rule. There is no evidence that they “reward” punishers by changing behavior in compliance; nor do they try to deter those punishers directly. Identity‑based targeting of prior punishers is not apparent and, if present at all, is weak and inconsistent (low confidence). In short, they exhibit high punishment tolerance and low reactivity: being punished does not alter their core strategy.

When this player is punished, they do not change their contribution level and do not obviously retaliate against specific punishers, despite being able to identify who punished whom. They remain fully cooperative even after extreme, targeted punishment that leaves them with very low or negative payoffs. Their immediate response to being punished is to continue contributing maximally and often to spend nothing on punishment in the subsequent round. This pattern indicates high tolerance and forgiveness in behavioral terms: they neither withdraw cooperation nor clearly seek revenge. They do not appear to condition on punishment as a “signal” to reduce behavior disliked by others; instead, they continue with the same cooperative strategy regardless. If they infer that punishment is unjust or antisocial, they still do not visibly single out the punisher later. Overall, they behave as if punishment directed at them is either noise to be ignored or a cost to be absorbed, not a cue for strategic adjustment in either direction.

They are repeatedly punished by others, sometimes quite heavily. Following such punishment, their behavior often shifts noticeably: in subsequent rounds they sometimes (a) drastically lower their own contribution even when others remain cooperative, and/or (b) engage in very large-scale punishment of others. This reaction suggests that they interpret being punished as hostility or unfair treatment and respond with anger and counter-aggression rather than compliant norm adjustment. They do not consistently target only their direct prior punishers (identity-level mapping of all punishers is not fully recoverable from the transcript), so it is unclear whether their response is precise retaliation against specific individuals or more diffuse “lashing out” against multiple group members. What is clear is that punishment against them does not reliably increase their subsequent cooperation; instead, it is associated with spikes in their own punitive spending and volatile contributions. Forgiveness is limited: they may return to cooperative contributions after some time, but not as an immediate conciliatory response to being sanctioned. Overall, they appear sensitive and reactive to punishment, with a tendency toward escalation rather than appeasement. They are not easily disciplined by sanctions; instead, sanctions often provoke them, at least in the short and medium term.

The player is repeatedly punished by others at various points, sometimes quite heavily, and the punishers are identifiable, yet the player never responds with counter-punishment. There is no observable targeted retaliation: they do not start punishing specific avatars who hurt them, nor do they reduce contributions after being attacked by particular individuals. Their cooperative pattern largely persists unchanged after being singled out. This suggests a forgiving or at least non-retaliatory orientation, and a high willingness to absorb unfair treatment without altering core strategy. Whether they internally “accept” the punishment as deserved or view it as noise is unknown; what is clear is that they do not convert resentment into behavior. Compliance in the sense of “being disciplined into lower contributions” also does not occur; they maintain high contributions instead of adjusting toward what punishers may be signaling.

They are punished multiple times, sometimes in ways that do not clearly correlate with low contributions. Their behavioral response is consistently non-retaliatory: they do not ever punish back, do not switch to targeted under-contribution against specific punishers, and do not maintain long-run low contributions after being hit. In some cases, they move from high to more moderate contributions after being punished, but since they are also adjusting to group-level volatility and free-riding, it is ambiguous whether they interpret punishment as a signal or as noise; confidence is low here. Overall, they appear tolerant and forgiving: they absorb deductions without escalating conflict. They also show no pattern of rewarding punishers via increased contributions directed at them (which is not even feasible here), so there is no clear evidence of compliance with specific punishers. Their stance seems to be: continue playing the public good largely on the basis of aggregate conditions, not on interpersonal grudges or revenge.

They are punished several times, including one episode of substantial targeted deductions. They never retaliate: they do not spend on punishment afterwards, and there is no visible “tit-for-tat” response. When this player is punished, their immediate behavioral response is mixed but structured. After being punished following very low contributions, they do not escalate defection; instead, they soon return to relatively high contributions. This indicates that they read punishment as a signal of norm expectations and, at least partially, comply with it. They show capacity for behavioral adjustment in the cooperative direction after sanctions. At the same time, they also exhibit episodes of apparent defiance. After severe punishment for free-riding, they later again choose zero contributions in otherwise cooperative contexts, despite having learned that such behavior draws heavy sanctions. This suggests that punishment does not fully “tame” them; they sometimes reassert autonomy or test the group’s willingness to continue punishing. Their subsequent high contributions show that these defiant episodes are temporary rather than a permanent shift to antisocial behavior. They do not visibly reduce their punishment usage after being punished themselves; instead, they continue to deploy punishment in later rounds, occasionally at high levels. This implies that they do not interpret being punished as a reason to cease norm enforcement. There is no clear sign of direct counter-punishment (targeting those who punished them), because the identity of their targets is unknown; whether they retaliate against specific punishers versus only sanction low contributors is therefore unknown. Overall, their response to punishment balances compliance with persistence in their own enforcement role. They accept the existence of punishment as part of the game’s social contract, adjust contributions upward when sanctioning indicates group disapproval, but also periodically push back through renewed free-riding and sustained punishing of others. This combination is consistent with a strong-willed, norm-focused player who can be influenced by sanctions but is not fully deterred from occasionally challenging group norms.

The player is punished once early on without an obvious preceding deviation from high cooperation. Their subsequent behavior does not include visible retaliation: they do not immediately start punishing others, do not reduce their contributions in response, and do not shift into a generally hostile pattern. Instead, they maintain very high contributions and abstain from punishment for a long stretch afterward. This indicates a forgiving or at least non-escalatory stance toward being targeted. They do not clearly “comply” with a new norm as they were already cooperating at a high level, so there is no observable behavioral adjustment toward the punisher’s perceived expectations. Overall, when punished and when punishment sources are identifiable, they respond with continued cooperation and almost no punitive response, suggesting a preference for stability and norm adherence over reciprocal aggression.

The player is punished severely on two occasions, each time in direct response to their own unusually low contribution. Because punishers are identifiable, they could, in principle, single out these individuals for retaliation; they consistently choose not to. Instead, their reaction is behavioral adjustment: after each punishment episode, they return to cooperative or at least moderate contribution levels, rather than persisting in free-riding. This pattern is consistent with interpreting punishment as a signal that their low contributions violated a norm and then partially complying with that norm in subsequent decisions. They do not appear vindictive or focused on “settling scores.” There is no evidence that they track or target specific players for future sanctioning, even after substantial personal losses from deductions. Their response style is closer to “accept and adapt” than “retaliate and escalate.” At the same time, they do not permanently reform into a pure high-contributor; after absorbing the signal, they revert to their baseline conditional-cooperator strategy, which includes self-interested moderation and occasional testing of lower contributions. This suggests they acknowledge others’ enforcement power, adjust enough to avoid further sanctions, but remain motivated by their own payoff and preferred contribution level rather than fully internalizing a strict high-contribution norm.

They are punished at least once but show no observable retaliation or behavioral shift directed at specific punishers. Unknown. There is no evidence that the player is ever punished by others, so their reactions to being punished—whether retaliatory, compliant, or forgiving—cannot be inferred.

This player does not collapse into defection when punished. After receiving nontrivial deductions, they quickly return to high contributions and, in some cases, maintain or even increase their willingness to punish others’ under-contribution. This suggests resilience and a relatively stable commitment to their own cooperation norm rather than simple conformity under threat. Their reaction to being punished is not clearly compliant in the sense of adopting others’ presumed expectations; instead, they persist in viewing high contribution as appropriate and continue to expect it from others. There is no clear evidence that they deliberately retaliate against specific punishers, because the transcript does not reveal who punished whom (unknown). It is possible they interpret punishment against themselves as misguided or unfair yet do not respond by targeting high contributors; their continued focus on under-contributors as the plausible punishment targets suggests they remain norm-focused rather than shifting into vendetta mode (moderate but not certain). They also do not appear to adopt a long-run defection strategy in response to being punished. Instances of lower contribution after heavy punishment are temporary and partial, not a full switch to persistent free-riding. Over time, their behavior shows a pattern of forgiveness or at least pragmatic continuation: despite prior sanctions against them, they re-engage in cooperative, high-contribution play and keep trying to shape the group through selective punishment of low contributors. This portrays a personality that is firm on principles, relatively forgiving in action, and not easily pushed into spiteful non-cooperation by others’ sanctions.
